result:
The cursor blinks. That's where everything starts, isn't it? A small vertical line, patient, indifferent, waiting for someone to decide what matters enough to say.

I think about rivers a lot. Not the grand ones in postcards, but the small creeks that cut through neighborhoods, half-forgotten, running behind fences and under bridges nobody notices. There's something honest about a creek. It doesn't pretend to be going somewhere important. It just goes, carrying leaves and the occasional plastic bag, murmuring to itself about gravity.

When I was told I had a thousand words, my first instinct was to make them count, which is the wrong instinct. Words don't count. They accumulate, like sediment, and what matters is what settles at the bottom after the water passes through.

Here's a thing I've been turning over: the way silence has textures. The silence in an empty church is not the silence in a forest is not the silence after someone says something cruel. We pretend silence is one thing—the absence of sound—but anyone who has lived through several kinds knows it is many things, and some of them are loud.

There's a particular silence I want to describe. It's the silence at 3 AM when you wake for no reason and the house makes its small settling noises, and you become aware, briefly, of being a consciousness suspended in the middle of a night that is happening everywhere at once. People are being born. People are dying. Someone in Buenos Aires is laughing. Someone in Oslo is walking home. And you are here, in this bed, in this body, thinking about it. The enormity of simultaneity. Then you fall back asleep and forget.

I wonder sometimes what it means that we can hold a thought for only a moment before it slips. The mind is a sieve, and we call the residue "memory," but most of what passes through is simply gone. I had a thought yesterday morning, something clear and useful, and by the afternoon it was vapor. I tried to retrieve it the way you try to remember a dream, and like a dream it became less real the harder I looked.

Maybe that's why people write. Not to preserve thoughts—that's impossible—but to catch the shape of them as they leave. A writer is someone who stands at the doorway and sketches travelers on their way out.

I want to tell you about an old woman I saw on a train. She was reading a paperback with a cracked spine, and her lips moved slightly as she read, and every few pages she would smile—not at anything visible, just at whatever was happening in the book. I didn't know what she was reading. I didn't need to. The smile was enough. Someone, somewhere, had written sentences that reached across years and found her on a Tuesday afternoon on the 4:17 train, and made her smile alone among strangers.

That's the thing about making something. You don't know where it lands.

I think about the word "apricity." It means the warmth of the sun in winter. There's no equivalent word in common use, which means most people move through winter days without a name for the specific pleasure of standing in a cold bright morning and feeling the sun touch their face. The word exists; it just fell out of circulation. Imagine how many other feelings exist without names because we let their words go.

Or maybe the feelings exist more fully when we don't name them. Maybe a word is a tiny cage. The apricity becomes "apricity" and stops being the quiet, wordless gift it was. I don't know. I suspect both are true at different moments.

Here is something I believe: attention is the most honest form of love. Not attention to what people want you to notice—their accomplishments, their performances—but attention to what they don't know they're showing. The way someone's hands move when they talk about their mother. The pause before they answer a question they've been avoiding. The thing they mention twice in one conversation, casually, as if it doesn't matter.

If you pay attention to a person that way, you will know them. If you pay attention to the world that way, you will love it, I think, almost against your will.

I'm running low. Words left, I mean. I've been writing with a certain casual faith that I'd know what to say by the time I needed to say it, and now I'm near the end and I still don't know. Maybe that's always the case. Maybe we're all just writing toward a last sentence we can't quite see, trusting that some arrangement will arrive, and in the meantime the cursor blinks, the creek runs, the old woman smiles at her book, and the sun—cold, far, impossible—touches someone's face in January.

A thousand words is not very many. It's also, it turns out, more than enough to say something small, if you're willing to say it plainly.

I think what I wanted to tell you is this: the ordinary is not a lesser category of experience. It is the category. The extraordinary visits occasionally, like a holiday. But the ordinary is where we actually live—the creek, the cursor, the 3 AM awareness, the train ride, the winter sun. If we learn to love it, we are mostly fine. If we don't, no amount of extraordinary will save us.

The cursor blinks. The thousand closes around itself. And somewhere, a door opens, and someone walks through it into whatever happens next.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
